agent_output:
# Domain Analysis: Driver Earnings Transparency Feature

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **compensation visibility** business process — the real-time disclosure of how a driver's total earnings are composed. This is not merely a UI feature; it is a **compliance-critical system** that intersects labor law, worker classification, and co-determination rights across jurisdictions. Getting this wrong doesn't just create a bad UX — it creates **legal liability**.

### Ubiquitous Language

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **Base Pay** | The guaranteed compensation a driver receives for being available/working, independent of deliveries completed. In employee contexts (CA), this is the hourly wage floor. In contractor contexts (ID), this may be a per-task minimum or absent entirely. |
| **Per-Delivery Bonus** | Variable compensation tied to each completed delivery. Must be clearly distinguished from base pay to avoid wage-law violations in employee jurisdictions. |
| **Tips** | Gratuities from customers. Legally, tips belong to the driver in all three jurisdictions. They must NEVER be counted toward base pay minimums. |
| **Deductions** | Amounts subtracted from gross earnings (e.g., platform fees, equipment rental, insurance contributions, tax withholdings). The legal permissibility and required disclosure of deductions varies by jurisdiction. |
| **Gross Earnings** | Base Pay + Per-Delivery Bonus + Tips, before deductions. |
| **Net Earnings** | Gross Earnings − Deductions. The "take-home" number. |
| **Worker Classification** | The legal relationship (employee vs. independent contractor) that determines which labor laws apply. This is NOT a platform choice — it is a legal determination per jurisdiction. |
| **Earnings Period** | The time window over which earnings are aggregated for display (shift, day, week, pay period). Must align with local payroll cycle requirements. |
| **Works Council Approval** | In Germany, formal consent from the Betriebsrat (employee representative body) required before deploying systems that monitor or display individual worker performance/compensation data. This is a **hard legal gate**, not a courtesy. |

### Bounded Context

**Driver Compensation & Compliance** — this sits at the intersection of the Payroll, Compliance, and Driver Experience bounded contexts. The earnings *calculation* belongs to Payroll; the *display rules* (what must/can/cannot be shown) belong to Compliance; the *presentation* belongs to Driver Experience.

---

## Business Rules

| # | Rule | Invariant? | Testable Assertion |
|---|------|:----------:|-------------------|
| 1 | **Tips must never be included in base pay calculations** | ✅ YES | Given a driver in any jurisdiction, when base pay compliance is checked, then tip amounts are excluded from the minimum wage/base pay floor calculation. |
| 2 | **California drivers must see a complete earnings breakdown every pay period** | ✅ YES | Given a CA-classified driver, when they view earnings, then base pay, per-delivery bonus, tips, and itemized deductions are all visible — no component may be hidden or aggregated into "other." |
| 3 | **Germany: feature MUST NOT go live without documented works council approval** | ✅ YES | Given the Germany jurisdiction, when the feature is deployed, then a `works_council_approval` record exists with status `approved` and a date **before** the deployment date. Deployment without this is **illegal**. |
| 4 | **Deductions must be itemized, not lumped** | ✅ YES (CA) / ❌ No (ID) | Given a CA driver, when deductions are displayed, then each deduction has a label, amount, and legal basis. In Indonesia, itemization is recommended but not legally required. |
| 5 | **Earnings data must reflect real-time or near-real-time state** | ❌ Soft | Given a driver who completes a delivery, when they check earnings within 5 minutes, then the delivery's compensation components are reflected. (Exact SLA is a product decision, but staleness erodes trust.) |
| 6 | **Worker classification determines which display rules apply** | ✅ YES | Given a driver, when their earnings view is rendered, then the compliance rules applied match their jurisdiction's worker classification — never the other jurisdiction's rules. A CA employee must never see a contractor-style display that omits legally required fields. |
| 7 | **Net earnings must equal gross earnings minus itemized deductions exactly** | ✅ YES | Given any earnings display, when net earnings is shown, then `net = base_pay + per_delivery_bonus + tips - sum(deductions)` holds to the cent. Any discrepancy is a **trust-destroying bug**. |
| 8 | **Currency and locale must match the driver's operating jurisdiction** | ✅ YES | Given a driver in Indonesia, when earnings are displayed, then amounts are in IDR with Indonesian locale formatting. No USD or EUR leakage. |
| 9 | **Historical earnings must be immutable once a pay period closes** | ✅ YES | Given a closed pay period, when earnings are displayed, then the values match the finalized payroll record exactly. Retroactive adjustments must appear as separate line items in the *current* period, not as silent edits to past periods. |
| 10 | **Indonesia drivers may see a simplified view, but must never see LESS than what local law requires** | ❌ Soft | Given an Indonesian contractor driver, when earnings are displayed, then at minimum the agreed-upon per-task rate and platform fee are visible. Full transparency is a product choice here, not a legal mandate. |

---

## Business Impact

### Financial
- **California**: Non-compliance with wage transparency laws exposes the platform to class-action lawsuits (penalties of $100/employee for first violation, $200+ for subsequent, plus attorney fees under PAGA). The financial exposure scales with driver count — potentially millions.
- **Germany**: Deploying without works council approval can result in injunctions halting the feature and fines. More critically, it poisons the labor relationship.
- **Indonesia**: Minimal legal financial risk, but transparency drives driver retention — reducing churn saves recruitment costs (estimated $200-500 per driver replacement in emerging markets).
- **ROI**: Transparency features consistently correlate with 10-15% improvement in driver satisfaction scores, which reduces churn.

### Operational
- Requires a **jurisdiction-aware rendering engine** — not just i18n, but fundamentally different display logic per jurisdiction. This is a compliance requirement, not a nice-to-have.
- Germany rollout is **blocked** until works council process completes (typically 4-12 weeks). The feature must support jurisdiction-level feature gating.
- Real-time earnings requires reliable event sourcing from the delivery completion pipeline. Stale data undermines the entire value proposition.

### User Impact
- Drivers gain trust and feel fairly compensated when they can verify their earnings. This is the #1 driver satisfaction lever in gig platforms.
- Poor implementation (wrong numbers, delayed updates, confusing labels) is **worse than no feature** — it actively breeds distrust.
- Drivers in California will compare displayed earnings to their pay stubs. Any discrepancy, even a rounding error, triggers complaints and potential legal claims.

### Risk Profile
| Risk | Severity | Likelihood |
|------|----------|------------|
| Deploying in Germany without works council approval | **Critical** | Medium (easy to overlook in global rollout) |
| Counting tips toward CA minimum wage floor | **Critical** | Low (but catastrophic if it happens) |
| Stale/incorrect earnings data displayed | **High** | Medium (depends on pipeline reliability) |
| Deduction not itemized in CA | **High** | Medium |
| Currency/locale mismatch | **Medium** | Low |

### Stakeholders Affected

| Stakeholder | Impact |
|-------------|--------|
| **Drivers (all jurisdictions)** | Primary beneficiary. Increased trust, ability to verify compensation. |
| **CA Drivers specifically** | Legal right to this information. Feature fulfills a compliance obligation. |
| **German Works Council** | Must be consulted and must approve. Failure to engage is a legal violation AND a relationship violation. |
| **Legal/Compliance team** | Owns the jurisdiction-specific display rules. Must sign off on each jurisdiction's implementation. |
| **Payroll team** | Source of truth for earnings data. Must ensure data pipeline accuracy. |
| **Product team** | Owns the UX. Must balance "show everything" (compliance) with "don't overwhelm" (usability). |
| **Finance team** | May face increased disputes/reconciliation workload if data is inaccurate. |
| **Customers (indirect)** | Tip transparency may influence tipping behavior. |

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### Core Scenarios

**AC-1: California full breakdown**
- **Given** a driver classified as employee in California
- **When** they view their current earnings
- **Then** they see: base pay (with hours worked), per-delivery bonus (with delivery count), tips (itemized per delivery), and each deduction (labeled with type and legal basis), and net earnings — all in USD

**AC-2: Germany pre-approval gate**
- **Given** the Germany jurisdiction has no approved works council record
- **When** the feature deployment pipeline runs for Germany
- **Then** the feature remains disabled for all German drivers, and an alert is raised to the compliance team

**AC-3: Germany post-approval**
- **Given** a works council approval record exists with status `approved`
- **When** a German driver views their earnings
- **Then** they see a breakdown compliant with the approved display format (as negotiated with the works council — the council may require specific formatting or additional fields)

**AC-4: Indonesia contractor view**
- **Given** a driver classified as independent contractor in Indonesia
- **When** they view their earnings
- **Then** they see at minimum: per-task earnings, platform service fee, tips, and net payout — in IDR

**AC-5: Tip isolation**
- **Given** a driver in California with base pay of $15/hr and $20 in tips
- **When** minimum wage compliance is calculated
- **Then** the $20 in tips is NOT included in the $15/hr base pay figure

**AC-6: Mathematical integrity**
- **Given** any driver viewing their earnings
- **When** they sum the displayed components
- **Then** `base_pay + per_delivery_bonus + tips - deductions = net_earnings` holds exactly (no floating-point display errors)

**AC-7: Real-time update**
- **Given** a driver who just completed a delivery
- **When** they refresh their earnings within 5 minutes
- **Then** the completed delivery's earnings components appear in the breakdown

**AC-8: Historical immutability**
- **Given** a closed earnings period from last week
- **When** the driver views that period's breakdown
- **Then** the displayed values are identical to the finalized payroll record and cannot differ from a previously viewed state

### Edge Cases

| Scenario | Expected Behavior |
|----------|-------------------|
| Driver works a shift with zero deliveries (CA) | Base pay (hourly) still displays; per-delivery bonus shows $0.00; net earnings reflects base pay minus any deductions |
| Tip is added retroactively (customer tips after delivery) | Tip appears in the *current* earnings view, attributed to the original delivery; does NOT alter a closed period |
| Deduction exceeds gross earnings (e.g., equipment damage) | Net earnings may display as negative; system must NOT hide this — driver must see it with explanation |
| Driver transfers between jurisdictions mid-period | Earnings split by jurisdiction; each segment follows its own compliance rules |
| Works council revokes approval in Germany | Feature must be immediately disable-able per jurisdiction without affecting other regions |
| Platform fee changes mid-shift (Indonesia) | New rate applies only to deliveries after the change; previous deliveries show the old rate |
| Currency conversion for cross-border drivers | Each earning component displayed in the jurisdiction's local currency; no implicit conversion |

---

## Confidence Assessment

| | |
|---|---|
| **Confidence Level** | **82%** |
| **High Confidence Areas** | California wage transparency requirements (well-established law); tip isolation rules (universal); mathematical integrity requirements; Germany works council requirement (clear legal mandate) |
| **Low Confidence Areas** | Exact works council negotiation outcomes for Germany (display format may be constrained by what they approve); Indonesia-specific labor regulations for contractor transparency (local law is less prescriptive — need local counsel confirmation); exact SLA for "real-time" (product decision, not domain rule) |
| **Assumptions** | (1) Worker classification is already correctly determined per jurisdiction and is available as driver metadata. (2) Payroll system is the authoritative source for earnings calculations. (3) Works council process has not yet begun in Germany. (4) The platform does not currently display any earnings breakdown (greenfield feature). (5) No other jurisdictions are in scope for initial rollout. |

---

## Rejection Reasons

None at this stage — this is a greenfield analysis, not a review of an implementation. However, I will **reject any implementation** that:

| Potential Violation | Business Rule Broken |
|---|---|
| Ships a single "one-size-fits-all" earnings view across all jurisdictions | Rule #6 — jurisdiction-specific compliance is non-negotiable |
| Launches in Germany without a works council approval gate | Rule #3 — this is illegal, full stop |
| Includes tips in the base pay line item or calculation | Rule #1 — violates CA labor law and universal tip ownership principles |
| Shows a single "Total Deductions" line without itemization in California | Rule #4 — CA requires itemized deductions |
| Allows silent edits to closed period earnings | Rule #9 — immutability of finalized payroll is a trust and audit invariant |
| Displays earnings in the wrong currency for the jurisdiction | Rule #8 — erodes trust and may violate local consumer protection norms |

---

**Bottom line:** This is not a "nice-to-have transparency feature" — in California, it's a **legal obligation**; in Germany, it requires **institutional consent before deployment**; in Indonesia, it's a **competitive advantage**. The implementation must be jurisdiction-aware at its core, not as an afterthought. Any architecture that treats compliance display rules as "configuration" rather than "first-class domain logic" will drift into violations.

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This feature encodes the **compensation visibility** business process — the real-time disclosure of how a driver's total earnings are composed. This is not merely a UI feature; it is a **compliance-critical system** that intersects labor law, worker classification, and co-determination rights across jurisdictions. Getting this wrong doesn't just create a bad UX — it creates **legal liability**.
